1:
"And as he went out of the temple, one of his disciples saith unto him, Master, see what manner of stones and what buildings are here!" The witnesses note the disciples' admiration of the temple and Christ's prophecy of its ruin. Robertson: "Matthew 24:1 and Luke 21:5 tell of the fact of the comment, but Mark alone gives the precise words. Perhaps Peter himself (Swete) was the one who sought thus by a pleasant platitude to divert the Teacher's attention from the serious topics of recent hours in the temple." Barnes and Clarke cite Josephus on the prodigious stones: "fifty feet long, twenty-four broad, and sixteen in thickness." Ellicott notes the "stones of Herod's Temple were of sculptured marble," and the buildings included "columns, chambers, porticos that were the votive offerings of the faithful."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the departure and prophecy theologically. Bede: "after the founding of the Church of Christ, Judaea was to be punished for her treachery, the Lord fitly, after praising the devotedness of the Church in the person of the poor widow, goes out of the temple, and foretold its coming ruin." He adds that "when the Lord left the temple, all the edifice of the law and the framework of the commandments were destroyed." Theophylact: the disciples "wondered, that such numerous and beautiful buildings were to be destroyed."

The puritan Henry draws the lesson on misplaced admiration: "How apt many of Christ's own disciples are to idolize things that look great... they court him to be as much in love as they were with the stately structure." And Christ's reply shows "how little Christ values external pomp, where there is not real purity... He looks with pity upon the ruin of precious souls, and weeps over them... but we do not find him look with any pity upon the ruin of a magnificent house." Ellicott notes the disciples "were still measuring things by their quantity and size. They admired the goodly stones more than the widow's mite."

The witnesses agree: as Christ left the temple for the last time, a disciple admired its magnificent stones and buildings — and Christ, valuing not external pomp where purity is wanting, would foretell its utter ruin (v.2). The departure marks the abandonment of the temple and the old covenant; the disciples' admiration, a worldly measuring by size that Christ would correct. The scene opens the great Olivet Discourse, in which Christ unfolds the destruction of Jerusalem, the trials of his Church, and his coming again.
2:
"And Jesus answering said unto him, Seest thou these great buildings? there shall not be left one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down." The witnesses note the completeness of the prophesied destruction. Robertson: "Jesus fully recognizes their greatness and beauty. The more remarkable will be their complete demolition (loosened down). Only the foundation stones remain." Barnes cross-references Luke 19:44. Gill notes the reply, though the comment came from one, was "made to him."

The thoroughness of the ruin — "not one stone upon another" — the witnesses note as a precise and stupendous prophecy. Theophylact addresses an objection: "some may endeavour to prove that Christ's words were false, by saying that many ruins were left, but this is not at all the point... it is related, that Aelius Adrian [Hadrian] overturned the city and the temple from the foundation, so that the word of the Lord here spoken was fulfilled." The prophecy was fulfilled in the Roman destruction of A.D. 70 and the later razing under Hadrian.

Henry (under v.1) drew out the force: "Much of the strength of the temple lay in the largeness of the stones, and if these be thrown down, no footstep, no remembrance, of it will remain. While any part remained standing, there might be some hopes of the repair of it; but what hope is there, when not one stone is left upon another?" The very magnificence the disciples admired would be utterly overthrown.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, acknowledging the temple's greatness, foretold its complete demolition — not one stone left upon another. The prophecy, stupendous in its day when the temple seemed eternal, was precisely fulfilled in the Roman destruction of A.D. 70 and the later razing under Hadrian. The completeness of the ruin — no stone, no footstep, no remembrance remaining — marked the final end of the old covenant's centre and the judgment on a nation that had rejected its Messiah. The verse, with its exact and astonishing prediction, both displays Christ's foreknowledge and sets the disciples wondering, prompting their question (vv.3-4) about the time and the sign of so great a calamity. (The Cyril fragment here, on the two names Jesus and Christ, is an off-topic catechetical aside.) The temple the disciples gazed on in admiration would become a heap, that the true temple, Christ and his Church, might be revealed.
3:
"And as he sat upon the mount of Olives over against the temple, Peter and James and John and Andrew asked him privately." The witnesses note the setting and the four named disciples. Ellicott marks the detail: "The view which the position commanded, and which St. Mark alone mentions, made all that followed more vivid and impressive. It may well have been at or near the very spot at which, a few days before, He had paused as He beheld the city and wept over it (Luke 19:41)." On the names: "given by St. Mark only... the only instance in which the name of Andrew appears in conjunction with the three who were on other occasions within the inner circle." Robertson: "In full view of the temple about which they had been speaking... they ask Jesus a bit to one side, though probably all the rest drew up as Jesus began to speak this great eschatological discourse."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the setting symbolically and survey the discourse. Bede: "The Lord sits upon the mount of Olives, over against the temple, when He discourses upon the ruin and destruction of the temple, so that even His bodily position may be in accordance with the words... pointing out mystically that, abiding in peace with the saints, He hates the madness of the proud. For the mount of Olives marks the fruitful sublimity of the Holy Church." Bede surveys the false christs (Simon Magus, Acts 8:10), the wars, and warns the apostles not to flee Judaea prematurely, "because the end was not to come at once, nay was to be put off for forty years."

Augustine frames the whole discourse: Christ "tells them of things which were from that time forth to have their course; whether He meant the destruction of Jerusalem which occasioned their question, or His own coming through the Church... or the end itself, in which He will appear to judge the quick and the dead." This three-fold reference — Jerusalem, the Church's age, the final end — runs through the patristic and modern reading alike.

The witnesses agree: on the Mount of Olives, in full view of the doomed temple, the four inner disciples (named only by Mark) asked Christ privately about his prophecy — opening the great discourse. The setting, opposite the temple where Christ had wept over the city, makes the prophecy vivid. The fathers read the discourse as embracing the destruction of Jerusalem, the trials of the Church through her age, and the final coming — a layered prophecy whose threads, as later voices agree, are not always sharply distinguished.
4:
"Tell us, when shall these things be? and what shall be the sign when all these things shall be fulfilled?" The witnesses note the disciples' two-fold question and the interpretive crux it opens. Robertson: "They asked about the when and the what sign. Matthew 24:3 includes the sign of thy coming and the end of the world, showing that these tragic events are brought before Jesus by the disciples." He frames the key issue: "Jesus blended in one picture his death, the destruction of Jerusalem within that generation, the second coming and end of the world typified by the destruction of the city. The lines between these topics are not sharply drawn in the report and it is not possible for us to separate the topics clearly." Ellicott notes Mark's "vaguer words for the more definite sign of Thy coming, and of the end of the world in Matthew 24:3," perhaps characteristic of a Gentile Gospel. Wesley: "Two questions are here asked; the one concerning the destruction of Jerusalem: the other concerning the end of the world."

The disciples, the witnesses note (with Henry, under v.1 and v.24), confounded the two events — the destruction of the temple and the end of the world — supposing the temple must stand as long as the world. This confusion shapes the whole discourse, in which Christ answers both, the destruction of Jerusalem serving as a type and foreshadowing of the final coming.

Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, reads the question as a providential guarding against deception: "since we are looking for Christ, therefore, that we may not die deceived and be led astray by that false Antichrist, the Apostles, moved by the divine will, address themselves by a providential arrangement to the True Teacher, and say, Tell us, when shall these things be... put us therefore on our guard." Yet on the timing, Cyril cautions: "as to the time let no one be curious. For it is not for you to know times or seasons."

The witnesses agree: the disciples' two-fold question — when, and what sign — opens the discourse, but their confounding of the temple's destruction with the end of the world shapes the answer. Christ would address both, weaving together the near judgment on Jerusalem and the final coming, so that (as Robertson and Wesley note) the threads are layered and not sharply separated. The destruction of Jerusalem, within that generation, becomes the type and pledge of the world's end; and the discourse, throughout, presses watchfulness rather than curiosity about times. (The Cyril fragment also touches the cautionary theme of not declaring the time.)
5:
"And Jesus answering them began to say, Take heed lest any man deceive you." The witnesses note that Christ opens his answer not by satisfying curiosity but by warning against deception. Robertson: "This word (our 'planet') is a bold one. This warning runs through the whole discussion. It is pertinent today after so many centuries... All through the ages in spite of the words of Jesus men have sought to apply the picture here drawn to the particular calamity in their time." Ellicott notes the discourse "obviously made a deep impression... and so it passed from mouth to mouth, but probably it was not committed to writing till the events which it foretold came within the horizon."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.5-13) draws out the warnings. First, against deceivers: "Take heed lest any man deceive you, lest, having found the true Messiah, you lose him again in the crowd of pretenders... When many are deceived, we should thereby be awakened to look to ourselves." Second, against being disturbed by wars (vv.7-8): "Let it be no surprise to you... Let it be no terror to you, as if your interest were in danger... Those that despise the smiles of the world... may despise the frowns of the world." Third, against being drawn away by sufferings (v.9): "it is through many tribulations that you must enter into the kingdom of God."

The opening warning, the witnesses note, sets the tone of the whole discourse: Christ directs the disciples' consciences rather than their curiosity. Henry: "Our Lord Jesus, in reply to their question, sets himself, not so much to satisfy their curiosity as to direct their consciences; leaves them still in the dark concerning the times and seasons... but gives them the cautions which were needful."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the opening of the discourse proper. The witnesses agree: Christ begins his answer with a warning — "Take heed lest any man deceive you" — directing the disciples' consciences rather than feeding their curiosity. The caution against deception runs through the whole discourse and through every age, for in times of calamity men are prone to follow false christs and false prophets. Christ leaves the times and seasons in the dark, but arms his people against the dangers they will face: deceivers, alarming wars, and persecutions. The repeated "take heed" (vv.5, 9, 23, 33) is the refrain of the discourse, pressing watchfulness over speculation. The verse opens the great prophecy with the practical and abiding lesson that the believer's first concern, amid the signs of the times, is not to calculate the end but to guard against being led astray.
6:
"For many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ; and shall deceive many." The witnesses note the false christs who would arise. Ellicott: "Literally, I am He. The word Christ being a necessary inference from the context." Barnes cross-references Acts 5:36-39 (Theudas and Judas) and 1 John 4:1 (try the spirits). Clarke notes the manuscript addition "the Christ."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers identify the deceivers. Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic: "This has happened in part: for already Simon Magus has said this, and Menander, and some others of the godless leaders of heresy; and others will say it in our days, or after us." Bede (under v.3) had named Simon Magus as the first, to whom the Samaritans said, "This man is the great power of God" (Acts 8:10). The false christs arose both before the destruction of Jerusalem (deceiving the Jews) and after, in the heretics who opposed the Church.

Henry (under v.5) drew out the warning: "Many shall come in my name (not in the name of Jesus), but saying, I am the Christ, and so claiming the dignities which I only am entitled to. After the Jews had rejected the true Christ, they were imposed upon, and so exposed by many false Christs, but never before; those false Christs deceived many; Therefore take heed lest they deceive you." The rejection of the true Christ left the Jews open to a succession of impostors.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: false christs would arise, claiming the dignity of the Messiah and deceiving many — a judgment on the Jews who rejected the true Christ, and a danger to the Church through the heretics. The deceivers came both before Jerusalem's fall (the many who claimed to be the Christ and led the people astray) and after (Simon Magus, Menander, and the heresiarchs). The verse fulfils the opening warning (v.5): having rejected the true Messiah, the nation was exposed to a crowd of pretenders, and the disciples are charged to take heed. The pattern, the fathers note, recurs in every age, culminating in the great deceiver, Antichrist. The believer's safeguard is to hold fast the true Christ, once found, against all who would usurp his name. Christ's warning both foretells the impostors and arms his people against them.
7:
"And when ye shall hear of wars and rumours of wars, be ye not troubled: for such things must needs be; but the end shall not be yet." The witnesses note the warning against alarm at wars and the assurance that they are not yet the end. Robertson catalogues the fulfilment: "Already there were outbreaks against the Jews in Alexandria, at Seleucia with the slaughter of more than fifty thousand... Caligula, Claudius, Nero will threaten war before it finally comes with the destruction of the city and temple by Titus in A.D. 70." Lightfoot notes how "the traditions of the scribes affrighted the nation with the report of Gog and Magog, immediately to go before the coming of the Messiah" — against which Christ warns "be not troubled."

Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, reads it as "a second sign": "And ye shall hear of wars and rumours of wars... Does nation rise up against nation and kingdom against kingdom, or no?... These things have already come to pass." The wars are signs, but not the immediate end.

Henry (under v.5) drew out the four-fold caution: "Let it be no surprise to you; you are bid to expect it... Let it be no terror to you, as if your interest were in danger... Let it not be looked upon as an omen of the approaching period of the world, for the end is not yet... Let it not be looked upon as if in them God has done his worst; no, he has more arrows in his quiver." The disciples may "enjoy a holy security and serenity of mind, when all about them is in the greatest disorder."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ warns his disciples not to be troubled at wars and rumours of wars, for such things must come but are not yet the end. Against the popular Jewish expectation that wars and the upheaval of Gog and Magog would immediately usher in the Messiah, Christ teaches that these are but the beginning, not the consummation. The disciples are to expect such commotions without surprise, terror, or false inference that the end is at hand. The verse both foretells the wars that preceded Jerusalem's fall (and recur through every age) and steadies the believer's heart against alarm — for the people of Christ may enjoy serenity amid the world's disorder, knowing that God overrules all and that the end comes in his appointed time, not at the noise of every war. The warning guards against the perennial error of reading every calamity as the immediate end.
8:
"For nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom: and there shall be earthquakes in divers places, and there shall be famines and troubles: these are the beginnings of sorrows." The witnesses note the catalogue of calamities and the phrase "beginnings of sorrows." Robertson documents the fulfilment between A.D. 30 and 70: "an earthquake in Crete (A.D. 46 or 47), at Rome (A.D. 51), at Apamaia in Phrygia (A.D. 60), at Campania (A.D. 63)," and "four famines during the reign of Claudius," one in Judea in A.D. 44 (Acts 11:28). Barnes explains the phrase: "the word in the original importeth the pains of a woman in travail." The calamities are not the end but the first pangs, like the onset of birth-pains.

Lightfoot draws out the prophetic background from Isaiah 66:7-8, which the Jews themselves applied to the Messiah's coming amid Jerusalem's travail: "In the same hour that the destruction of the Temple was, Israel cried out as though she were bringing forth." The image of birth-pangs frames the calamities as the painful prelude to a new order.

Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, joins it to the call to watch: "And there shall be famines and pestilences and earthquakes in divers places... and fearful sights from heaven, and mighty storms. Watch therefore, He says; for ye know not at what hour your Lord doth come."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wars, earthquakes, famines, and troubles are "the beginnings of sorrows" — the first birth-pangs, not the end. The calamities, abundantly fulfilled in the decades before Jerusalem's fall (and recurring through every age), are the painful prelude to the judgment and, beyond it, to the world's end. The image of travail-pains frames them as the onset of a new order, born through suffering. Christ teaches his disciples to see these troubles not as the consummation but as its early signs, that they may neither be terrified by them nor mistake them for the end. The verse both foretells the convulsions that would precede Jerusalem's destruction and prepares the disciples for the persecutions that would fall on themselves (vv.9-13) — the pangs intensifying as the day drew near. Watchfulness, not alarm, is the fitting response to the beginnings of sorrows.
9:
"But take heed to yourselves: for they shall deliver you up to councils; and in the synagogues ye shall be beaten: and ye shall be brought before rulers and kings for my sake, for a testimony against them." The witnesses note the persecutions foretold and the comfort attached. Ellicott marks "the emphatic repetition of the warning [take heed to yourselves] is peculiar to St. Mark." Barnes and Clarke detail the courts: the "councils" are the Sanhedrin and local synagogue courts; the "rulers and kings" are Roman officers and the Herodian tetrarchs. Robertson notes the persecution fulfilled in Acts — Peter, John, Stephen, Paul brought before councils and governors.

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the persecution as both judgment and testimony. Bede: "the greatest cause of destruction to the Jewish people was, that after slaying the Saviour, they also tormented the heralds of His name and faith with wicked cruelty." Yet Christ comforts the disciples that "even after the casting away of the Jews, companions in their joy and heavenly kingdom should not be wanting." Theophylact reads "for a testimony against them" as "a judgment beforehand against them, that they might be inexcusable, in that though the Apostles were labouring for the truth, they would not join themselves to it." He notes "for My sake gave them no small consolation, in that they were about to suffer for His sake."

Henry (under v.5) drew out the comfort amid the trouble: the disciples' "being brought before governors and kings shall be for a testimony of them... it shall give you an opportunity of preaching the gospel to those before whom you are brought as criminals." Thus Paul before Felix, Festus, Agrippa, and Nero bore witness to Christ.

The witnesses agree: Christ forewarns his disciples of persecution — delivered to councils, beaten in synagogues, hauled before rulers and kings — yet attaches comfort: it is "for my sake," and "for a testimony." The sufferings, fulfilled in the apostles' trials (Acts), would be both a judgment leaving their persecutors inexcusable and an opportunity to preach Christ before the great. The repeated "take heed to yourselves" (peculiar to Mark) presses watchful endurance. The verse turns the prospect of persecution from a terror into a calling: to suffer for Christ's name is honour, and to be dragged before the powers is to gain a pulpit. The trials of the heralds, the fathers note, were among the causes of Jerusalem's ruin and the means of the gospel's spread.
10:
"And the gospel must first be published among all nations." The brief verse — given by Mark alone in this form — notes the universal spread of the gospel before the end. Robertson: "This only in Mark. It is interesting to note that Paul in Colossians 1:6, 23 claims that the gospel has spread all over the world. All this was before the destruction of Jerusalem." Gill: there was "a necessity of the promulgation of it by the will of God, the command and commission of Christ; and for the gathering in of the Jews, that were the elect of God, among all nations of the world, especially in the Roman empire; and that first, or before the destruction of Jerusalem." Barnes cross-references Matthew 28:19 and Revelation 14:6.

The "must" the witnesses note as the divine necessity. The gospel's spread to all nations was the appointed work that must go forward before the end — whether the near end of Jerusalem or the final end of the world. Theophylact (under v.9) had added the comfort: "that they might not think that their preaching should be impeded by troubles and dangers, He adds: And the Gospel must first be published among all nations." The persecutions would not stop the gospel but spread it.

Henry (under v.5) drew out the comfort to suffering preachers: "The gospel shall, for all this, be published among all nations... It is comfort to those who suffer for the gospel, that, though they may be crushed and borne down, the gospel cannot; it shall keep its ground, and carry the day."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which Mark gives in his own characteristic form. The witnesses agree: the gospel must first be published among all nations before the end — a divine necessity that no persecution could thwart. The spread of the gospel through the Roman world before A.D. 70 (as Paul attests, Colossians 1:6, 23) fulfilled this in its first reference; and its universal proclamation remains the appointed work before the final end. The "must" marks the sovereign purpose of God, which uses even the persecutions of the heralds to scatter the seed wider. The verse comforts the suffering Church: though her preachers be crushed, the gospel cannot be stopped but keeps its ground and carries the day. The worldwide publication of the good news is both the divine condition of the end and the assurance that the powers of opposition, however fierce, cannot overthrow the purpose of God to gather his elect from every nation.
11:
"But when they shall lead you, and deliver you up, take no thought beforehand what ye shall speak... but whatsoever shall be given you in that hour, that speak ye: for it is not ye that speak, but the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the promise of the Spirit's help before tribunals. Robertson clarifies the scope: "Jesus is not here referring to preaching, but to defences made before these councils and governors. A typical example is seen in the courage and skill of Peter and John before the Sanhedrin in Acts... There is no excuse here for the lazy preacher who fails to prepare his sermon out of the mistaken reliance upon the Holy Spirit." Barnes: "This is a full promise that they should be inspired; and consequently their defences recorded in the Acts of the Apostles are the words of the Holy Ghost." Ellicott notes Mark's "more definite term [Holy Ghost]... may, probably, be connected with St. Peter's habitual language."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the design: "to relieve the disciples from that anxiety which interferes with the cheerful discharge of our duty, when we doubt our inability to sustain the burden. Not that he wishes us to fall asleep in indolent security, for nothing is more advantageous than to have such a consciousness of our weakness as produces humility and excites to prayer. But Christ advises us to cast all our cares into the bosom of our Father." He notes the promise is "not that Christ will deliver his people from death... but that to confound their adversaries," the Spirit guiding their tongues "so as to enable them to make a wise and suitable reply."

The Gloss (under v.9) explained the comfort: "lest after hearing that they were to be brought before kings and rulers, they should fear that their want of science and eloquence should render them unable to answer, our Lord consoles them." The promise meets the disciples' natural fear of standing speechless before the great.

The witnesses agree: Christ promises that, when his disciples are dragged before tribunals, they need take no anxious forethought, for the Holy Ghost will speak in them — a promise of inspired defence, fulfilled in the apostles' courage before the Sanhedrin and the Roman powers. The promise concerns defences under persecution, not ordinary preaching (which still requires diligent preparation). Its design is to relieve anxiety and excite trust, not to encourage indolence. The believer, conscious of weakness yet casting his care on the Father, is assured that in the hour of trial the Spirit will furnish a wise and confounding answer. The verse turns the terror of standing before kings into a promise of divine eloquence, the Spirit of the Father speaking through his persecuted children.
12:
"Now the brother shall betray the brother to death, and the father the son; and children shall rise up against their parents, and shall cause them to be put to death." The witnesses note the betrayal even by closest kin. Barnes: "The brother shall give up in a treacherous manner his brother to be put to death, on account of his attachment to Jesus. Through fear, in the hope of reward and the hatred of the gospel, he will overcome all the natural ties of brotherhood." Ellicott notes the literal rendering, "and the brother." Gill: "such should be the rage of men, particularly the Jews, against Christ and his Gospel, that those that were in the nearest relation... should betray and deliver up each other into the hands of the civil magistrate."

The betrayal by kindred the witnesses read as the depth of the persecution's evil. Barnes cross-references Micah 7:6, the prophecy of family division that Christ here applies to the gospel age. The hatred of Christ would prove so fierce that it would overcome the strongest natural affections — brother betraying brother, father the son, children their parents — turning the closest bonds into instruments of death.

Theophylact (under v.9) had noted Christ's purpose in foretelling it: "He also foretells to them a worse evil, that they should suffer persecution from their relations... And this He says, that on hearing it, they might prepare themselves to bear persecutions and ills with greater patience." The forewarning was to steel the disciples against even this bitterest of trials.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ foretells betrayal even by the closest kin — brother delivering brother, father son, children parents — to death, for the sake of his name. The hatred of the gospel would prove so fierce as to overcome the strongest natural affections, fulfilling Micah's prophecy of family division (Micah 7:6). The forewarning was to prepare the disciples to bear with patience even this bitterest of trials, when those they most trusted for protection would become their accusers. The verse reveals both the depth of the world's enmity against Christ and the cost of discipleship, which may sever the dearest earthly ties. Christ, who himself was betrayed, prepares his followers for betrayal; and the love that binds believers to him must, when tested, prove stronger than the love of kindred. The bitterest persecution comes not from strangers but from those of one's own house.
13:
"And ye shall be hated of all men for my name's sake: but he that shall endure unto the end, the same shall be saved." The witnesses note the universal hatred and the promise to the persevering. Gill: hated "not only of your friends and relations of your countrymen the Jews; but of all men, the generality of men, in all nations of the world." Robertson parses the key phrase: "remaining under (endure) until the end... Here Jesus means final salvation (effective aorist future passive), not initial salvation." Barnes cross-references Luke 6:22 and John 17:14 (the world's hatred), and Daniel 12:12 and Revelation 2:10 (the promise to endurance).

The hatred "for my name's sake" the witnesses note as the ground of comfort. Theophylact (under v.9): "the being hated for Christ's sake is a sufficient reason for suffering persecutions patiently, for it is not the punishment, but the cause, that makes the martyr." The disciples were hated not for any wrong but for Christ — and so their suffering was honour, not shame.

The promise "he that shall endure unto the end... shall be saved" the witnesses read as the crown of perseverance. Henry (under v.5): "Perseverance gains the crown. The salvation here promised is more than a deliverance from evil, it is an everlasting blessedness, which shall be an abundant recompence for all their services and sufferings." Theophylact had called it "no small comfort amidst persecution."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the section on persecution. The witnesses agree: the disciples would be hated by all men for Christ's name, yet he who endures to the end shall be saved. The universal hatred, being for Christ's sake, is itself a ground of comfort, for it is the cause, not the suffering, that makes the martyr. And against the prospect of hatred stands the promise of final salvation to the persevering — a reward that infinitely outweighs all the suffering. The verse both forewarns of the world's enmity and crowns endurance with everlasting blessedness. Perseverance, not mere beginning, gains the crown; the believer is called not to a brief profession but to a steadfast holding-out unto the end, through hatred, betrayal, and trial. The promise of salvation to the enduring is the anchor of the persecuted Church, turning the prospect of universal hatred into the path to glory.
14:
"But when ye shall see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet, standing where it ought not, (let him that readeth understand,) then let them that be in Judaea flee to the mountains." The witnesses note the sign of Jerusalem's destruction and the command to flee. Robertson: "Matthew 24:15 has standing in the holy place... Mark personifies the abomination as personal (masculine), while Luke 21:20 defines it by reference to the armies (of Rome, as it turned out). So the words of Daniel find a second fulfilment, Rome taking the place of Syria." Augustine reconciles the Gospels: "Matthew says, standing in the holy place; but... Mark has expressed the same meaning... But Luke, in order to shew that the abomination of desolation happened when Jerusalem was taken, in this same place gives the words... when ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies."

The fathers identify the abomination variously. Bede: "it may either be said simply of Antichrist, or of the statue of Caesar, which Pilate put into the temple, or of the equestrian statue of Adrian." He records the historical fulfilment: "all the Christians who were in that province, warned by the prophecy, fled far away... and retiring beyond Jordan, remained for a time in the city of Pella." Theophylact: "the entrance of enemies into the city by violence." Cyril of Jerusalem and Bede also extend it to the future Antichrist (2 Thessalonians 2).

The puritan Henry (covering vv.14-23) reads it of the Roman armies: "These were the abomination of desolation, which the Jews did abominate, and by which they should be made desolate... This army stood where it ought not, in and about the holy city." He stresses the urgency of flight: "there would be no safety any where but by quitting the country, and that with all possible expedition... If he can save his life, let him reckon it is a good bargain, though he can save nothing else."

The witnesses agree: the "abomination of desolation" (Daniel 9:27), standing where it ought not, was the sign for the Christians to flee Judaea — fulfilled when the Roman armies compassed Jerusalem (Luke 21:20), and the believers, warned, escaped to Pella before the city fell. The fathers read a layered fulfilment: the Roman desolation, and beyond it the future Antichrist. The parenthesis "let him that readeth understand" calls for discernment of the prophecy. The verse both foretells the precise sign that would save the Christians from the catastrophe and, in the fathers' wider reading, points to the final tribulation. The command to flee, urgently obeyed, preserved the Church from the ruin that fell on the unbelieving nation.
15:
"And let him that is on the housetop not go down into the house, neither enter therein, to take any thing out of his house." The brief verse — with Gill, Barnes, and Clarke pointing to the Matthew parallel — presses the urgency of flight. Gill notes the housetop, where one might be "either for diversion or devotion," with its flat roof and outside stair. The command is that one who spies the approaching enemy from the roof should flee at once, not even descending to gather his goods.

The urgency the witnesses (with Henry, under v.14) draw out: "If he be on the house-top, trying from thence to discover the motions of the enemy, and spies them coming, let him not go down, to take any thing out of the house, for it will occasion his losing of time, which is more precious than his best goods, and will but encumber him, and embarrass his flight." The flight must be immediate and unencumbered; to pause for possessions is to risk one's life.

The Gloss (under v.14) had read it spiritually: "for it is a desirable thing to be saved even naked from such a destruction." Better to escape with nothing than to perish for the sake of one's goods. The command teaches the surpassing value of life over property in the hour of judgment, and (in the spiritual reading of Bede, under v.14) the call of the soul that "rises above carnal deeds" not to "come down to the base acts of his former conversation."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command presses the utter urgency of flight — the man on the housetop, spying the enemy, must flee at once, not descending even to save his goods. To pause for possessions is to lose the precious time on which life depends. The verse teaches that in the hour of judgment life infinitely outweighs property; better to escape naked than to perish encumbered. The literal command, which preserved the fleeing Christians of A.D. 70, carries the abiding lesson (Bede) that the soul risen to spiritual life must not come down again to its former carnal ways, nor let the love of earthly things hinder its escape. When destruction is at the door, the time to flee is now, and everything that would delay the flight must be left behind. The surpassing worth of salvation over all earthly goods is the heart of the warning.
16:
"And let him that is in the field not turn back again for to take up his garment." The brief verse — with Gill and Robertson — continues the command to immediate flight. Gill notes the man "at work, in any sort of business there" in the field. Robertson observes the grammatical parallel with Matthew: "Here Matthew 24:18 has [in the field], showing identical use of [the preposition] with accusative and with the locative." The man labouring in the field, having laid aside his outer garment for work, must not even turn back to fetch it, but flee as he is.

The command, like that of v.15, presses the surpassing urgency of escape. Henry (under v.14): "If he be in the field, and there discover the approach of the enemy, let him get away as he is, and not turn back again, to take up his garment. If he can save his life, let him reckon it is a good bargain, though he can save nothing else, and be thankful to God, that, though he is cut short, he is not cut off." Even the garment, a necessary possession, must be abandoned if fetching it would delay the flight.

The pairing of the man on the housetop (v.15) and the man in the field (v.16) the witnesses note as covering both town and country, both rest and labour: wherever one is when the sign appears, the response must be the same — immediate, unencumbered flight. The repetition drives home the lesson that nothing, however valuable or necessary, is worth the loss of the precious moments on which life depends.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the man in the field must not even turn back for his garment but flee as he is — the same urgency as the man on the housetop (v.15), now pressed on the labourer in the country. The command teaches that in the hour of judgment escape must be immediate and unencumbered; even a necessary possession must be left behind rather than risk delay. The pairing of town and country, rest and labour, shows that wherever one is overtaken by the sign, the response is the same: flee at once, save your life, count all else well lost. The literal warning, which preserved the Christians of A.D. 70, carries the abiding lesson that when God's judgment is at the door, nothing earthly — not even the clothes on one's back — must be allowed to hinder the soul's flight to safety. The value of life and salvation eclipses every possession.
17:
"But woe to them that are with child, and to them that give suck in those days!" The brief verse — with only Gill in the Catena — laments the special hardship of mothers in the time of flight. Gill: "who because of their burdens, would be very unfit for, and very incapable of fleeing with any haste; and therefore very liable to fall into the hands of the enemy, and become their prey." The pregnant and the nursing, hindered by their condition, could neither flee swiftly nor easily, and so were peculiarly exposed to the calamity.

The woe the witnesses (with Henry, under v.14) read as a tender compassion: "Woe to them that are with child, that dare not go into strange places, that cannot shift for themselves, nor make haste as others can. And woe to them that give suck, that know not how either to leave the tender infants behind them, or to carry them along with them." The very comforts of motherhood — the child in the womb, the babe at the breast — become, in the hour of flight, grievous burdens.

Henry drew the sober reflection: "Such is the vanity of the creature, that the time may often be, when the greatest comforts may prove the greatest burthens." What in ordinary times is the chief joy of a woman's life becomes, in the day of calamity, her heaviest impediment. The Gloss (under v.14) had noted this as one of the "impediments to flight" — the obstacle of having children to carry.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woe pronounced on pregnant and nursing women laments their peculiar hardship in the time of flight, when their condition would prevent swift escape and expose them and their children to the enemy. The verse breathes a tender compassion for those least able to flee, and a sober reflection that the greatest comforts of life — the child in the womb, the babe at the breast — may in the day of judgment become the heaviest burdens. The literal woe, fulfilled in the horrors of the siege of Jerusalem (when, Theophylact notes under v.14, famine drove some mothers to dreadful extremities), carries the abiding lesson that earthly attachments, however precious, may hinder us in the hour of trial. Christ's compassion extends even to foreseeing and lamenting the hardest cases of the coming calamity; his warning is also a mourning over those who would suffer most.
18:
"And pray ye that your flight be not in the winter." The brief verse — with Gill and Ellicott — adds a further circumstance to be prayed against. Gill: "When days are short, roads bad, the weather inclement; and when to lodge in mountains, is very incommodious, and uncomfortable." Flight in winter would compound the hardship — short daylight for travel, foul roads, bitter cold, and no shelter in the mountains to which they must flee.

Ellicott marks a characteristic omission: "Note St. Mark's omission of nor on the Sabbath day, which is prominent in St. Matthew's report, as characteristic of a Gospel for Gentile readers." Where Matthew (writing for Jews) adds the Sabbath difficulty, Mark (writing for Gentiles, to whom the Sabbath restriction would mean little) gives only the winter.

The command to pray about the circumstances of the flight the witnesses (with Henry, under v.14) read as teaching submission and petition even amid foretold trouble: "If there be no remedy but that trouble must come, yet we may desire and pray that, if it be God's will, the circumstances of it may be so ordered as to be a mitigation of the trouble; and when things are bad, we ought to consider they might have been worse. It is bad to be forced to flee, but it would have been worse if it had been in the winter." Though the calamity was certain, its circumstances were still matter for prayer.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ bids his disciples pray that their flight be not in winter — when the cold, the short days, the bad roads, and the want of shelter would aggravate the hardship. The command teaches that even foretold and certain trouble may be the subject of prayer as to its circumstances; though the calamity must come, its severity may be mitigated by God's ordering, and we may rightly ask that it be so. Mark's omission of Matthew's "nor on the Sabbath day" suits his Gentile readers. The verse displays both Christ's tender concern for the comfort of his fleeing people and the propriety of praying about the manner of unavoidable trials. When trouble is certain, we may still pray that it come in the gentlest form; and we should reckon, when it comes, that it might have been worse. Prayer has its place even within the bounds of a fixed decree.
19:
"For in those days shall be affliction, such as was not from the beginning of the creation which God created unto this time, neither shall be." The witnesses note the unequalled tribulation. Gill catalogues it: "what with the close siege of the Romans; the fury of the zealots, and seditious; the rage of different parties among the Jews themselves; the ravage of the sword... together with dreadful plagues and famines." Ellicott notes "the fuller form which replaces St. Matthew's from the beginning of the world." Robertson notes the amplification "which God created" added to the quotation from Daniel 12:1.

The fathers (under v.14) read the tribulation on two levels. Augustine: "Josephus, who has written the history of the Jews, relates that such things were suffered by this people, as are scarcely credible, wherefore it is said, not without cause, that there was not such tribulation... But although in the time of Antichrist there shall be one similar or greater, we must understand that it is of that people, that it is said that there shall never happen such another." The unequalled affliction is that of the Jewish people in A.D. 70; the future tribulation under Antichrist is of a different reference.

Wesley raises the wider question: "May it not be doubted, whether this be yet fully accomplished? Is not much of this affliction still to come?" — reading the prophecy as reaching beyond Jerusalem to the final tribulation. Cyril of Jerusalem similarly applies "such as hath not been from the beginning of the world" to the great tribulation under Antichrist, confined by God's mercy "to a few days."

The witnesses agree: the affliction of those days would be unequalled in all history — fulfilled, in its first reference, in the horrors of the siege of Jerusalem (A.D. 70), which Josephus records as scarcely credible. The fathers and later voices read a layered fulfilment: the unique tribulation of the Jewish nation, and (Wesley, Cyril) the future tribulation under Antichrist. The amplified phrase "from the beginning of the creation which God created" (from Daniel 12:1) underscores the singularity of the calamity. The verse both foretells the specific horror that would fall on Jerusalem for rejecting her Messiah and, in the wider reading, points to the final great tribulation. The unequalled affliction marks the severity of the judgment; yet it would be mercifully shortened for the elect's sake (v.20).
20:
"And except that the Lord had shortened those days, no flesh should be saved: but for the elect's sake, whom he hath chosen, he hath shortened the days." The witnesses note the mercy of the shortening and its purpose — the elect. Gill: the Lord "had determined that those days of affliction should be but few, and not last long; that the siege should not be longer continued." Robertson notes "whom he chose... in Mark alone. Explains the sovereign choice of God in the end by and for himself." Wesley defines the elect: "The Christians: whom he hath chosen — that is, hath taken out of, or separated from, the world, through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth."

The shortening "for the elect's sake" the witnesses read as the mercy of God amid judgment. Theophylact (under v.14): "if the Roman war had not been soon finished, no flesh should be saved; that is, no Jew should have escaped; but for the elect's sake, whom He hath chosen, that is, for the sake of the believing Jews, or who were hereafter to believe, He hath shortened the days... for God foresaw that many Jews would believe after the destruction of the city; for which reason He would not suffer the whole race to be utterly destroyed."

Henry (under v.14) drew out the double mercy: "He shortened the days; he let fall his controversy before he had made a full end. As a church and nation the ruin was complete, but many particular persons had their lives given them for a prey, by the storm's subsiding when it did... It was for the elects' sake that those days were shortened; many among them fared the better for the sake of the few among them that believed in Christ."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the days of the unequalled tribulation were mercifully shortened, for had they continued, no flesh would have survived — and the shortening was "for the elect's sake," the believing or yet-to-believe remnant whom God had chosen. The mercy amid judgment displays God's care for his elect, for whose sake even the wicked are spared a fuller destruction. The verse reveals the sovereign purpose of God working through the calamity: the war was cut short that the chosen might be preserved and that many might yet come to faith. Mark's "whom he hath chosen" underscores the divine election that governs even the timing of judgment. The shortening of the days is the thread of mercy running through the unequalled affliction, the elect being the reason the storm subsided when it did. God remembers his own in the midst of wrath.
21:
"And then if any man shall say to you, Lo, here is Christ; or, lo, he is there; believe him not." The witnesses note the renewed warning against false christs, now (in the fathers' reading) referring to the time of Antichrist. Theophylact: "After that the Lord had finished all that concerned Jerusalem, He now speaks of the coming of Antichrist... But when He says, then, think not that it means immediately after these things are fulfilled about Jerusalem... He speaks indefinitely." The "then" is not chronological succession but a shift to the later time.

The fathers expound the deceiving signs (v.22) and the elect's security. Augustine: "then shall Satan be unchained, and work through Antichrist in all his power, wonderfully indeed, but falsely." He weighs whether the "lying wonders" are false phantoms or true marvels turning men to lies, concluding "they shall be deceived by those signs and wonders who deserve to be deceived." Gregory expounds the "if it were possible, even the elect" (v.22): "if they are elect, it is not possible; and if it is possible, they are not elect. This doubt therefore in our Lord's discourse expresses the trembling in the mind of the elect... for those who are chosen to remain firm are to be tempted to fall by the signs of the preachers of Antichrist."

Bede (under v.21) distinguishes the references: "Some however refer this to the time of the Jewish captivity... but during the siege of the city there was no Christian to whom the Divine exhortation, not to follow false teachers, could apply. Wherefore it is better to understand it of heretics... the first of whom was Simon Magus, but that last one, greater than the rest, is Antichrist." Cyril of Jerusalem connects it to 2 Thessalonians 2: "the man of sin be revealed... so that he sitteth in the temple of God, shewing himself that he is God."

The witnesses agree: Christ renews the warning against false christs, the fathers reading it now of the time of Antichrist, when Satan unchained will work lying wonders to deceive, "if it were possible, even the elect." The "then" marks a shift from Jerusalem to the later tribulation. The deceiving signs will be so plausible as to test the very chosen — yet (Gregory) the elect, by definition, cannot finally be seduced, though they tremble. The verse both warns against the impostors who would arise (before and after Jerusalem, and supremely in Antichrist) and assures the security of the elect, who are kept by the power of God. The believer's safeguard, against even the most plausible deceptions, is to hold fast the true Christ, who comes not secretly ("lo, here") but visibly, in the clouds (v.26).
22:
"For false Christs and false prophets shall rise, and shall shew signs and wonders, to seduce, if it were possible, even the elect." The brief verse — with Gill and Robertson — describes the deceiving power of the impostors. Gill: "As there did, both before and after the destruction of Jerusalem." Robertson parses the purpose: "With a view to leading off... Matthew 24:24 has [a stronger construction], so as to lead off."

The signs and wonders the fathers (under v.21) take seriously as a real or apparent display of power. Augustine weighed whether they are "false" because phantoms deceiving the senses, or true marvels turning men to lies; either way, "they shall be deceived by those signs and wonders who deserve to be deceived." The deceivers would not merely speak but display apparent miracles, so plausible as to threaten even the chosen.

The phrase "if it were possible, even the elect" the witnesses (with Gregory, under v.21) read as both the measure of the danger and the security of the elect. Gregory: "if they are elect, it is not possible; and if it is possible, they are not elect." The deceptions would be so powerful that, were the elect not kept by God, they would be seduced; yet precisely because they are elect, they cannot finally fall. Henry (under v.14): "They shall seduce, if it were possible, the very elect; but it is not possible to seduce them; the election shall obtain... The Lord knoweth them that are his."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: false christs and false prophets would arise with signs and wonders so plausible as to threaten even the elect — yet the very phrasing "if it were possible" both measures the danger and guarantees the elect's security, for they cannot finally be seduced. The deceivers, both before and after Jerusalem's fall and supremely in Antichrist, would display apparent miracles to lead men astray; and only those "who deserve to be deceived" (Augustine) would be taken. The verse warns the Church of the most dangerous deceptions — miraculous in appearance, aimed at the very chosen — while assuring that the foundation of God stands sure: "The Lord knoweth them that are his" (2 Timothy 2:19). The elect tremble, watch, and are kept; the reprobate, lacking love of the truth, are given over to the lie. The warning calls for vigilance grounded in the security of God's keeping.
23:
"But take ye heed: behold, I have foretold you all things." The witnesses note the renewed warning and the sufficiency of Christ's forewarning. Ellicott: "The repetition of the warning word, as in Mark 13:9, is peculiar to St. Mark." Robertson: "Gullibility is no mark of a saint or of piety. Note emphatic position of you. Credulity ranks no higher than scepticism. God gave us our wits for self-protection. Christ has warned us beforehand." Gill: "though the purposes of God, concerning the salvation of his elect, are infrustrable... yet it becomes them to be on their guard against every deception."

The forewarning "I have foretold you all things" the witnesses read as both a means of safety and an argument against further speculation. Henry (under v.14): "I have foretold you of this danger, that, being fore-warned, you may be fore-armed; I have foretold all things which you needed to have foretold to you, and therefore take heed of hearkening to such as pretend to be prophets, and to foretel more than I have foretold. The sufficiency of the scripture is good argument against listening to such as pretend to inspiration."

Henry also reconciles the warning with the elect's security: "Christ knew that they were of the elect, who could not possibly be seduced, and yet he said to them, Take heed. An assurance of persevering, and cautions against apostasy, will very well consist with each other... God will keep them, but they must keep themselves." The warning is the appointed means by which the elect are preserved.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the section on deceivers. The witnesses agree: Christ renews the warning — "take ye heed" — and grounds it in his complete forewarning: "I have foretold you all things." The believer's safety lies in heeding Christ's word, which has told all that is needful; gullibility is no virtue, and credulity no better than scepticism. The forewarning is both the means of the elect's preservation (caution and assurance consisting together — God keeps them, yet they must keep themselves) and an argument against listening to those who pretend to foretell more than Christ has revealed. The sufficiency of Christ's word is the guard against every deception. The verse presses watchful use of the means God has appointed, the repeated "take heed" (Mark's refrain) calling the Church to vigilance grounded in the all-sufficient forewarning of her Lord.
24:
"But in those days, after that tribulation, the sun shall be darkened, and the moon shall not give her light." The witnesses divide over whether this marks the second coming or (figuratively) the fall of Jerusalem and its order. Robertson cautions: "These figures come from the prophets (Isaiah 13:9f.; Ezekiel 32:7f.; Joel 2:1f.). One should not forget that prophetic imagery was not always meant to be taken literally, especially apocalyptic symbols. Peter in Acts 2:15-21 applies the prophecy of Joel about the sun and moon to the events on the day of Pentecost." Gill reads it of the time "after the destruction of the city and temple of Jerusalem."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.24-27) reads it of the second coming: "These verses seem to point at Christ's second coming, to judge the world; the disciples, in their question, had confounded the destruction of Jerusalem and the end of the world... this mistake Christ rectifies, and shows that the end of the world... shall be after that tribulation, and not coincident with it." He takes the darkening literally: "The sun shall be darkened, and the moon shall no more give her light; for they shall be quite outshone by the glory of the Son of man." Wesley agrees: "in those days — which immediately precede the end of the world: after that tribulation."

Cyril of Jerusalem reads it of the world's renewal: "this world passeth away, that the fairer world may be made manifest," citing Isaiah's "the heaven shall be rolled together as a scroll; and all the stars shall fall." The fathers and later voices alike take the cosmic signs as marking the end and the coming of the Judge.

The witnesses agree on the imagery's force while differing on its precise reference: the darkening of sun and moon, drawn from the prophets (Isaiah, Ezekiel, Joel), marks a great divine intervention. Some (Henry, Wesley, Cyril) read it of the literal end of the world and Christ's second coming, the heavenly lights outshone by his glory; others (Robertson) caution that apocalyptic imagery need not be literal, and that such language sometimes figures great upheavals (as Peter applied Joel to Pentecost). The reference — whether the second coming or, figuratively, the catastrophic end of the Jewish order — is part of the larger debate over the discourse (v.4). On either reading, the verse proclaims that "those days" will bring a shaking of the very heavens, a sign of God's coming in judgment and glory.
25:
"And the stars of heaven shall fall, and the powers that are in heaven shall be shaken." The brief verse continues the cosmic imagery of the great day. Robertson parses the construction: "Periphrastic future indicative... the stars shall be falling." Barnes supplies the prophetic background: Isaiah 13:10; 24:20, 23; Jeremiah 4:28; 2 Peter 3:10, 12; Revelation 6:12-14; 20:11. Gill offers a figurative reading: the falling stars are "the Jewish Rabbins and doctors, who fell off from the written word, and compiled their Misna, or oral law... and set it up above the Bible."

The falling stars and shaken powers the witnesses read (with the references) as the language of cosmic dissolution that the prophets use for great divine judgments. Bede (under v.21) gave the double sense: "the stars in the day of judgment shall appear obscure, not by any lessening of their own light, but because of the brightness of the true light, that is, of the most high Judge coming upon them; although there is nothing to prevent its being taken to mean, that the sun and moon with all the other heavenly bodies then for a time are really to lose their light." And: "The powers of heaven shall be shaken. What wonder is it that men should be troubled at this judgment, the sight of which makes the very Angelic powers to tremble?"

The "powers of heaven" the witnesses take variously — the heavenly bodies, or the angelic powers. Either way, the shaking marks the magnitude of the event: the very framework of the heavens convulsed at the coming of the Judge.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the falling stars and shaken powers continue the imagery of cosmic dissolution drawn from the prophets — the language Scripture uses for great divine judgments and supremely for the end. Whether the heavenly lights literally lose their light, or are outshone by the glory of the coming Judge (Bede), or the imagery figures the overthrow of an old order, the verse proclaims a shaking of the very heavens. The "powers of heaven" — the heavenly bodies or the angelic hosts — tremble at the magnitude of the event. The verse, with v.24, sets the cosmic stage for the appearing of the Son of Man (v.26): the framework of creation convulsed at his coming. The reference (literal end or figurative upheaval) remains debated (v.24), but the force is clear — the day of the Lord shakes heaven and earth, that all may know the Judge has come.
26:
"And then shall they see the Son of man coming in the clouds with great power and glory." The witnesses note the visible coming of the Son of Man. Ellicott: "Note the simpler form, which at once replaces and explains St. Matthew's the sign of the Son of Man." Barnes supplies the rich cross-references: Daniel 7:9-14; Matthew 16:27; 24:30; Mark 14:62; Acts 1:11; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; 2 Thessalonians 1:7, 10; Revelation 1:7. Gill, reading it of the judgment on Jerusalem, takes it figuratively: "Not in person, but in the power of his wrath and vengeance; of which the Jews then had a convincing evidence."

The witnesses divide, as throughout the discourse, over the reference. The puritan Henry (under v.24) reads it of the literal second coming: "the visible appearance of the Lord Jesus, to whom the judgment of that day shall be committed. Then shall they see the Son of man coming in the clouds. Probably he will come over that very place where he sat when he said this... He shall come with great power and glory... Every eye shall then see him." Gill, by contrast, reads it of the coming in judgment on Jerusalem.

The fathers (Augustine, under v.28) read the "coming in the clouds" both of the daily coming through the Church and of the final advent. Augustine (under v.21) had grounded the visible coming in Acts 1:11: "He shall so come in like manner as ye have seen Him go into heaven... He will come not only in the same body, but on a cloud, since He is to come as He went away." Cyril (under v.27) adds the sign of the Cross going before the King.

The witnesses agree on the majesty of the coming while differing on its reference: the Son of Man shall be seen coming in the clouds with great power and glory. Some (Gill) read it figuratively of Christ's coming in judgment on Jerusalem; most (Henry, the fathers, Cyril) read it of the literal second coming, when every eye shall see him (Revelation 1:7), as he was seen to ascend (Acts 1:11). The phrase, drawn from Daniel 7:13, proclaims the visible, glorious advent of the Judge. Mark's simpler form ("they shall see the Son of man") explains Matthew's "sign of the Son of Man." On either reading, the verse is the climax of the cosmic signs: the heavens shaken (vv.24-25) that the Son of Man may appear in power and glory — to judge, and to gather his elect (v.27).
27:
"And then shall he send his angels, and shall gather together his elect from the four winds, from the uttermost part of the earth to the uttermost part of heaven." The witnesses note the gathering of the elect. Robertson: "This is the purpose of God through the ages. From the uttermost part of the earth to the uttermost part of heaven... The Greek is very brief, from the tip of earth to the tip of heaven. This precise phrase occurs nowhere else." Ellicott notes "the absence of the trumpet, which is prominent in St. Matthew." Gill, reading it of the post-A.D. 70 spread, takes the angels as "the ministers of the Gospel to preach it, and plant more churches among the Gentiles, since that at Jerusalem was entirely broken up."

The fathers (under v.21) read the gathering of the elect richly. Bede: "By the four winds, He means the four parts of the world, the east, the west, the north, and the south. And lest any one should think that the elect are to be gathered together only from the four edges of the world... He has fitly added, From the uttermost part of earth, to the uttermost part of heaven... No one therefore shall be elect in that day who remains behind and does not meet the Lord in the air, when He comes to judgment." Theophylact: "the Angels go forth to gather together the faithful, who are chosen, that they may be carried into the air to meet Jesus Christ" (1 Thessalonians 4:17).

Henry (under v.24) drew out the completeness of the gathering: "He shall send his angels, and gather together his elect to him, to meet him in the air. They shall be fetched from one end of the world to the other, so that none shall be missing from that general assembly... so sure, so swift, so easy, shall their conveyance be, that there shall none of them miscarry."

The witnesses agree: at his coming the Son of Man sends his angels to gather his elect from the four winds, from earth's farthest bounds — the great ingathering of all the chosen to meet him. Whether read of the post-A.D. 70 gathering of the Gentiles into the Church (Gill) or, more commonly, of the final assembling of the elect at the second coming (Bede, Theophylact, Henry; 1 Thessalonians 4:17), the verse proclaims the completeness and certainty of the gathering: none of the chosen shall be missing, fetched swiftly and surely from the uttermost parts. The angels, the reapers of the harvest, gather the elect to meet the Lord in the air. The verse is the goal of the coming: not only judgment on the wicked but the safe ingathering of all God's people, the purpose of God through the ages brought to its consummation.
28:
"Now learn a parable of the fig tree; When her branch is yet tender, and putteth forth leaves, ye know that summer is near." The witnesses note the parable's lesson of reading the signs. Robertson: "Present middle participle, linear action. See on Matthew 24:32-36 for details." Ellicott notes a textual variant ("it is known"). Gill: Christ "was now upon the Mount of Olives, in one part of which fig trees grew in great plenty," the season (near Passover) being right for the budding.

The puritan Henry (covering vv.28-37) applies the parable to the destruction of Jerusalem: "When second causes begin to work, ye expect their effects in their proper order and time. So when ye see these things come to pass, when ye see the Jewish nation embroiled in wars, distracted by false Christs and prophets... then say that their ruin is nigh, even at the door." The signs Christ has given are like the budding fig tree, presaging the near approach of the calamity.

The fathers (under v.28) read the fig tree variously. Bede: "This fruitbearing of the fig tree may also be understood to mean the state of the synagogue, which was condemned to everlasting barrenness... But the Apostle has said that when the fulness of the Gentiles is come in, all Israel shall be saved." Theophylact: "as when the fig tree puts forth its leaves, summer follows at once, so also after the woes of Antichrist, at once, without an interval, shall be the coming of Christ." Pseudo-Jerome: "the summer at hand is the day of Judgment."

The witnesses agree: the parable of the fig tree teaches the disciples to read the signs — as the budding tree presages summer, so the signs Christ has given presage the near approach of the predicted events. Applied (Henry) to the destruction of Jerusalem, the signs of wars, false christs, and persecutions mark its ruin "even at the door." The fathers extend it to the synagogue's barrenness and Israel's future salvation (Romans 11), and to the coming of Christ after the woes of Antichrist. The verse turns the discourse from prophecy to application: the believer is to discern the times, reading the signs not with idle curiosity but with watchful readiness. As certainly as summer follows the budding fig, so surely will the predicted events come when their signs appear — and the wise will be prepared.
29:
"So ye in like manner, when ye shall see these things come to pass, know that it is nigh, even at the doors." The briefest verse — with only Gill's opening words in the Catena — applies the parable of the fig tree. Gill: "This is an accommodation of the parable to the present case." As the budding fig tree signals the nearness of summer, so the signs Christ has given signal the nearness of the predicted events.

The application the witnesses (with Henry, under v.28) draw out: "when ye see these things come to pass, when ye see the Jewish nation embroiled in wars, distracted by false Christs and prophets, and drawing upon them the displeasure of the Romans, especially when ye see them persecuting you for your Master's sake... then say that their ruin is nigh, even at the door." The signs are the certain harbingers of the event; when they appear, the disciples are to know that the predicted calamity is imminent.

Augustine (under v.28) noted that "it" (or "he") is nigh: "what does He refer to in the words, when ye shall see these things come to pass, but those things which He has mentioned above, amongst which it is said, And then ye shall see the Son of Man coming." The nearness is of the predicted events — the destruction of Jerusalem in its first reference, and (in the layered reading) the coming of the Son of Man.

The thin comment matches the shortest verse of the application. The witnesses agree: the parable is applied — when the disciples see the foretold signs come to pass, they are to know that the predicted event is "nigh, even at the doors." As the budding fig tree certainly heralds summer, so the signs of wars, false christs, and persecutions certainly herald the approaching calamity. Applied to the destruction of Jerusalem (its near reference), and in the layered reading to the coming of the Son of Man, the verse calls the believer to discern the times and to be ready. The certainty of the connection — sign and event, as inseparable as the fig's leaves and summer — presses watchfulness: when the signs appear, the day is at the door, and the wise will be prepared. The verse turns prophecy into practical alertness, bidding the disciples read the unfolding events as the sure pledge of the predicted end.
30:
"Verily I say unto you, that this generation shall not pass, till all these things be done." The witnesses note the solemn time-limit and debate the reference of "this generation." Gill: "not the generation of men, in general, or Jews in particular, nor of Christians; but that present generation of men, they should not all go off the stage of life" before the predicted events. Clarke gives both senses: "Generation signifies a period of a certain number of years... In Deuteronomy 1:35; 2:14, Moses uses the word to point out a term of thirty-eight years, which was precisely the number in the present case; for Jerusalem was destroyed about thirty-eight years after our Lord delivered this prediction. But as there are other events in this chapter, which certainly look beyond the destruction of Jerusalem... I should therefore prefer the translation... This very race of men [the Jews]." Wesley: "All these things — relating to the temple and the city."

The phrase the witnesses divide over. Some take "this generation" as the contemporary generation, fulfilled in the destruction of Jerusalem within forty years — the natural reading that ties the prophecy to A.D. 70. Others, troubled by the verses that "look beyond" Jerusalem, take it as the Jewish race, which should not pass away till all be fulfilled. Theophylact (under v.28) read it of "the generation of Christians... wishing to console His disciples, lest they should believe that the faith should fail."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, a key crux of the discourse. The witnesses agree on the solemn certainty of the prediction while differing on the scope of "this generation." Most naturally, it means the contemporary generation, and ties the central prophecy — the destruction of Jerusalem — to the forty years before A.D. 70, the precise span being a strong argument for the preterist reference of much of the discourse. Others, reading the chapter's wider scope, take it of the Jewish race (or of Christians), preserved till all be fulfilled. The interpretation belongs to the larger debate over the discourse (v.4): how much refers to Jerusalem and how much to the end. On any reading, the verse stamps the prophecy with the seal of certainty: within the appointed time, all these things must surely come to pass. The "verily I say unto you" underscores the infallibility of Christ's word, confirmed in v.31. (The Cyril fragment here, on eternal life, is a broader catechetical aside.)
31:
"Heaven and earth shall pass away: but my words shall not pass away." The witnesses note the supreme certainty of Christ's word. Gill explains the two possible senses: "either affirmatively, as what will be at the second coming of Christ; or comparatively, rather than any thing said by Christ should; and so shows the certainty and irreversibleness of his predictions." Barnes cross-references Isaiah 40:8, "the word of our God shall stand for ever."

The saying the witnesses read as the seal of certainty on the whole prophecy. Henry (under v.28): "Christ doth not speak these things, merely to frighten them; no, they are declarations of God's fixed purpose; Heaven and earth shall pass away, at the end of time; but my words shall not pass away, not one of these predictions shall fail of a punctual accomplishment." The stability of Christ's word exceeds that of the visible creation: heaven and earth, the most enduring things we know, shall pass away, but not one word of Christ shall fail.

Theophylact (under v.28) had drawn the comfort: "the immoveable elements shall first fail, before the words of Christ fail." The very framework of creation is less permanent than the word of the Son. The saying both confirms the predictions of the discourse and asserts the divine authority and eternity of Christ's word — a claim that, on the lips of a mere man, would be blasphemy, but on the lips of the Son is the plain truth.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ stamps his whole prophecy with the seal of supreme certainty — heaven and earth shall pass away, but his words shall not. The stability of his word exceeds that of the visible creation; the most enduring things we know shall perish before one word of Christ fails. The saying both confirms the infallibility of the predictions just given (v.30) and asserts the divine authority and eternity of Christ's word, echoing what Scripture says of the word of God (Isaiah 40:8). Whether "heaven and earth shall pass away" be read as a literal future fact or a comparative emphasis, the force is the same: nothing is more certain than the word of Christ. The verse is the anchor of the believer's confidence in all Christ has said — his promises and his warnings alike — for the word of the eternal Son cannot fail. (The Cyril fragment here, on the world's renewal, touches the passing of heaven and earth.)
32:
"But of that day and that hour knoweth no man, no, not the angels which are in heaven, neither the Son, but the Father." The witnesses face the great Christological crux: how can the Son not know? They divide between defending Christ's omniscience and explaining the limitation. Hilary insists Christ cannot really be ignorant: "If there are in Him all the treasures of knowledge, He is not ignorant of this day; rather we ought to remember that the treasures of wisdom in Him are hidden; His ignorance therefore must be connected with the hiding... For in all cases in which God declares Himself ignorant, He is not under the power of ignorance, but either it is not a fit time for speaking, or it is an economy of not acting." Theophylact reads it as a wise refusal: "If He had said, I know, but I will not reveal it to you, He would have saddened them not a little; but He acted more wisely, and prevents their asking."

The grammatical-historical and others address the genuine limitation. Barnes: "As a man, his knowledge must be finite, for the faculties of the human soul are not infinite... Why are not all these [human limitations]... as difficult as the want of knowledge respecting the particular time?" Wesley: "Neither the Son — not as man: as man he was no more omniscient than omnipresent. But as God he knows all the circumstances of it." Henry surveys the views: Archbishop Tillotson — "Christ, as God, could not be ignorant of any thing; but the divine wisdom which dwelt in our Saviour did communicate itself to his human soul, according to the divine pleasure, so that his human nature might sometimes not know some things." Robertson: "This disclaimer of knowledge naturally interpreted applies to the second coming." Some (Clarke, Macknight) propose the verb means "make known": "this secret was not to be made known... by the Son... but by the Father only."

Lightfoot, reading the day as Jerusalem's destruction, takes "that day" as the day "of the destruction of Jerusalem," and the saying as concealing its precise time. Gill agrees the reference is "of Jerusalem's destruction." Cyril of Jerusalem cites "none knoweth the Son but the Father" to affirm Christ's eternal generation and shared throne.

The witnesses agree the verse teaches the precise time is hidden — known to the Father alone — while differing on how to reconcile "neither the Son" with Christ's deity. The orthodox consensus: Christ, as God, knows all things; the limitation belongs to his human nature, which (as it grew in wisdom, Luke 2:52, and felt grief and fear) did not, by the divine pleasure, know this. The "ignorance" is either of his humanity, or an economy of not revealing. The verse both safeguards the mystery of the day's timing — against all date-setting — and, rightly understood, the integrity of Christ's two natures. The hiddenness of the hour is the ground of the call to watch (v.33): since none knows the day, all must be always ready.
33:
"Take ye heed, watch and pray: for ye know not when the time is." The witnesses note the practical conclusion drawn from the hidden hour: watchfulness and prayer. Ellicott: "Note once more the characteristic iteration of the warning [take ye heed]." Gill ties it to the preceding: the day and hour were not made known "that his people might be upon their guard, against false Christs, and prophets... and watch unto prayer." Barnes cross-references the parallel exhortations (Matthew 24:42; 25:13; Luke 21:34; Romans 13:11-12; 1 Thessalonians 5:6).

The threefold charge — take heed, watch, pray — the witnesses read as the fitting response to the hidden timing. Henry (under v.28): "As to both, your duty is to watch and pray. Therefore the time is kept a secret, that you may be engaged to stand always upon your guard... watch for his coming, that it may not at any time be a surprise to you, and pray for that grace which is necessary to qualify you for it, for ye know not when the time is; and you are concerned to be ready for that every day, which may come any day."
