The hiddenness of the hour, the witnesses note, is the very reason for constant watchfulness. Were the time known, men might delay their preparation; because it is unknown, they must be always ready. Hilary (under v.32) had noted that Christ, "lest He should be said to be ignorant from weakness... immediately added, Take ye heed, watch and pray." The uncertainty is providential, designed to keep the Church alert.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the hinge to the closing exhortation. The witnesses agree: the practical lesson of the hidden hour is watchfulness and prayer — "take ye heed, watch and pray, for ye know not when the time is." The very concealment of the day is designed to keep the Church always on guard, since what may come any day must be prepared for every day. The threefold charge presses both vigilance (watch) and dependence (pray for grace to be ready). The repeated "take heed" (Mark's refrain throughout the discourse) reaches its climax here. The verse turns the whole prophecy to its practical end: not speculation about times, but constant readiness for the coming of the Lord. Because the hour is unknown, the believer must live in perpetual expectation, watching and praying, that he be not found unprepared. The uncertainty is not a frustration but a spur to holy alertness.
34:
"For the Son of man is as a man taking a far journey, who left his house, and gave authority to his servants, and to every man his work, and commanded the porter to watch." The witnesses note the parable of the absent master and the watching servants. Barnes: "Our Saviour here represents himself as going away, leaving his household the Church, assigning to the apostles and all his servants their duty, and leaving it uncertain when he would return." Ellicott reads the porter specially: "who was specifically the gate-keeper or porter? The answer appears to be found in the promise of the keys of the kingdom that had been made to St. Peter (Matthew 16:19)... We may accordingly venture to trace in St. Mark's record, here as elsewhere, the influence of the Apostle. That word the porter was, he felt, meant for him." Robertson: "This Parable of the Porter is only in Mark."

The fathers (under v.32) expound the parable. Bede: "The man who taking a far journey left his house is Christ, who ascending as a conqueror to His Father after the Resurrection, left His Church, as to His bodily presence, but has never deprived her of the safeguard of His Divine presence." Gregory: "he gave his servants power over every work, when, by giving to His faithful ones the grace of the Holy Ghost, He gave them the power of serving every good work. He has also ordered the porter to watch, because He commanded the order of pastors to have a care over the Church... Not only, however, those of us who rule over Churches, but all are required to watch the doors of their hearts."

Clarke draws the full allegory: "1. That himself, ascended to heaven, is the man gone from home. 2. That believers collectively are his family. 3. That his servants are those who are employed in the work of faith. 4. That the porter represents the ministers of his Gospel, who should continually watch... 5. That every one has his own work... 6. That these servants and porters shall give an account."

The witnesses agree: the parable of the absent master and his servants teaches the duty of faithful watchfulness in Christ's bodily absence. Christ, ascended to the Father, has left his Church with work assigned to each and a charge to the porter (the pastors, or specially Peter) to watch. The servants must be diligent at their posts, ready for their Lord's return at an uncertain hour. The parable, unique to Mark and perhaps reflecting Peter's own memory of the charge to watch, presses the lesson of the whole discourse: each has his appointed work, all are to watch, and all must give account when the Master returns. The uncertainty of the return (v.35) makes constant readiness the only safety.
35:
"Watch ye therefore: for ye know not when the master of the house cometh, at even, or at midnight, or at the cockcrowing, or in the morning." The witnesses note the call to watch and the four watches of the night. Robertson: "The four watches of the night are named here: evening, midnight, cock-crowing, morning." Ellicott: "The four times correspond roughly to the four watches of the night, beginning at 9 P.M., 12, 3 A.M., 6 A.M. The words may be noted as having left... on St. Peter's mind, the impression that the promise of the coming of his Lord was undefined as to times or seasons, which is so prominent in 2 Peter 3. Each of the seasons named has had its counterpart, we may well believe, embracing many centuries of the world's history." Ellicott also corrects the rendering: "Better, the Lord of the house."

The four watches the witnesses (with Henry, under v.28) apply to the times of the Lord's coming: "We know not when he will come, just at what precise time; the Master of the house perhaps will come at even, at nine at night; or it may be at midnight, or a cock-crowing, at three in the morning, or perhaps not until six. This is applicable to his coming to us in particular, at our death, as well as to the general judgment." Henry adds: "Our present life is a night, a dark night... we know not in which watch of the night our Master will come, whether in the days of youth, or middle age, or old age."

The uncertainty of the watch the witnesses read as the ground of constant vigilance. Theophylact (under v.32) had applied the watches to the ages of life: "the end comes at even, when a man dies in old age; at midnight, when he dies in the midst of his youth; and at cockcrow, when our reason is perfect within us... the age of childhood is the morning. Now all these ages must look out for the end."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the call to watch is grounded in the uncertainty of the Lord's coming, which may fall in any of the four watches of the night. The believer knows not when the Master returns — whether at his death or at the general judgment, whether in youth, middle age, or old age — and so must watch in every watch. The naming of the four watches (a detail Mark records, perhaps reflecting Peter's memory) underscores that the time is undefined, as 2 Peter 3 affirms. The verse presses perpetual vigilance: since the coming may be at any hour, the servant must be ready at every hour. The night of this present life, dark and uncertain in its length, calls for constant watchfulness, that the Master, whenever he comes, may not find his servant sleeping (v.36). Watchfulness through every watch is the only safety against a sudden return.
36:
"Lest coming suddenly he find you sleeping." The brief verse states the danger to be guarded against. Barnes: "Find you sleeping. Inattentive to your post, neglecting your duty, and unprepared for his coming." Gill: "Unthought of, and unexpected, at an unawares." Clarke draws out the porter's peril: "A porter asleep exposes the house to be robbed, and well deserves punishment. No wonder that the man is constantly suffering loss who is frequently off his guard." Ellicott hears an echo: "something like a fragmentary reminiscence of that [parable] of the Wise and Foolish Virgins."

The danger of being found sleeping the witnesses read as the spiritual peril of unpreparedness. To sleep is to be inattentive to one's post, careless of duty, and unready for the Master's return — the opposite of the watchfulness commanded. Clarke draws the full lesson: the master "will certainly come to require this account at a time when men are not aware, therefore they should be always watchful and faithful... If, after all these warnings, the followers of God be found careless, their misery and condemnation must be great."

The suddenness of the coming the witnesses note as the reason sleeping is so perilous. Henry (under v.28): "His coming will indeed be coming suddenly; it will be a great surprise and terror to those that are careless, and asleep, it will come upon them as a thief in the night." The unexpectedness of the return makes constant readiness essential, for the sleeper has no warning.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the danger to be guarded against is being found sleeping at the Master's sudden return — inattentive, negligent, unprepared. The sleeping porter, exposing the house to robbery, deserves punishment; so the careless servant, found unready, faces misery and condemnation. The suddenness of the coming makes sleeping fatal, for it gives no warning; the Master comes "as a thief in the night," and only the watchful are safe. The verse states the negative of the command to watch (v.35): not merely to be busy, but never to be caught asleep. Spiritual sleep — carelessness, security, neglect of duty, forgetfulness of the coming — is the peril against which the whole discourse warns. The believer must so watch that, whenever the Lord comes, he be found awake and ready, not slumbering at his post. The certainty and suddenness of the return make perpetual wakefulness the only safety.
37:
"And what I say unto you I say unto all, Watch." The witnesses note the universal application of the closing charge. Gill: the words were spoken to "the four disciples, Peter, James, John, and Andrew; who came privately to him," yet are extended to all. Barnes: "This command was proper, not only for those who were expecting the calamities that were soon to come upon the Jews, but for all who are soon to die, and to go to the judgment. We know not the time of our death... The Son of man may come at any moment, and we should, therefore, be ready." Ellicott notes the impression the command made: "seen in a striking manner in the use of such names as Gregory, Vigilius, and the like."

The extension "unto all" the witnesses read as the universalizing of the charge to watch. Henry (under v.28): "What I say unto you four, I say unto all the twelve, or rather to you twelve, I say unto all my disciples and followers; what I say to you of this generation, I say to all that shall believe in me, through your word, in every age, Watch, watch, expect my second coming, prepare for it, that you may be found in peace, without spot, and blameless." The command, spoken to four, reaches every believer in every age.

Augustine (under v.32) had grounded the universal reach in the personal application of the day: "that day comes to each man when his day comes for departing from this life such as he is to be, when judged in that day; and for this reason every Christian ought to watch, lest the Advent of the Lord find him unprepared; but that day shall find him unprepared, whom the last day of his life shall find unprepared." Barnes makes the same connection: the day of death is, for each, the coming of the Lord.

The witnesses agree: the closing charge — "Watch" — spoken to the four, is extended to all, in every age. The command universalizes the lesson of the whole discourse: not only those awaiting Jerusalem's fall, but every believer awaiting death and judgment, must watch. Since the Lord may come at any moment — at the general judgment, or, for each, at the day of death — all must be always ready. The single word "Watch," repeated as the refrain and climax of the discourse, gathers up its whole teaching: be alert, be prepared, expect the coming, that you may be found in peace, without spot, and blameless. The charge reaches across the centuries to every follower of Christ, pressing the perpetual vigilance that is the only fitting response to a certain but unexpected return. The Olivet Discourse ends not in speculation about times but in the practical, universal, urgent command to watch.
